text: The surgical team places a medial camera port between two robotic ports and a peri-umbilical 15mm assistant port. The colonic reflection is incised, and the bowel is mobilized medially.

The surgeon dissects the upper pole of the kidney, identifying and elevating the ureter while lifting the kidney off the psoas muscle. Dissection continues towards the renal hilum, where the renal hilar vessels are exposed for potential clamping. The Girotis fascia is opened at the upper pole to release the adrenal gland and expose the cystic lesion.

Dense adherent inflammatory tissue is encountered and released during the dissection of the upper pole, exposing the upper pole of the kidney. The remaining kidney tissue is defatted to enhance exposure.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to delineate the junction between normal kidney tissue and the lesion. Monopolar cautery and sharp excision are employed to excise the lesion circumferentially without clamping.

The surgeon performs selective dissection of distal renal artery branches, identifying and clipping a segmental vessel to the kidney lesion. An anterior superior segmental artery supplying the non-viable portion of the kidney is divided.

The surgeon completes the transection of the kidney and places the specimen into an endocatch bag. To enhance visualization for renorrhaphy and closure of the collecting system, bulldog clamps are applied to temporarily occlude the renal vessels.

The surgeon over-sews the resection bed using 3-0 V-lock sutures anchored with clips, employing a sliding hemo-lock clip technique. The renal capsule is closed with interrupted sutures placed over hemostatic bolsters to ensure secure closure and hemostasis.
The surgeon places additional hemostatic agents around the surgical site. The bulldog clamps are removed from the renal vessels, and hemostasis is visually confirmed.
